title: Scaling Agents Without Creating Your Next Tech Legacy
gist: As enterprises race to deploy agentic AI, ungoverned agent sprawl and collapsing technology half-lives threaten to create a costly new legacy architecture. BCG argues CIOs have a six-to-twelve-month window to retrofit a scalable AI backbone built on four pillars (GenAI platform services, AI graduation pathways, continuous refactoring, and ITSM adapted for an Agent Development Life Cycle) before complexity outpaces their ability to govern it.

insights.claim: Two dynamics are already accelerating enterprise complexity risk as agentic AI scales: business-led agent sprawl, where experiments multiply into thousands of ungoverned agents, and an engineering productivity paradox, where AI-boosted software output piles on technical debt.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: The value case for agents and the risk case are two sides of the same adoption curve; investors should probe whether portfolio companies are tracking agent counts and technical debt, not just deployment wins.
insights.quote: business-led agent sprawl, where value proofs and experimentation multiply into thousands of ungoverned agents

insights.claim: Agentic tools and frameworks can become outdated in less than a year, forcing stable long-term platform partnerships to sit alongside a fast-moving agentic layer where tooling, models, and frameworks evolve in months.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Vendors selling durable platform lock-in versus fast-moving agentic tooling occupy different risk tiers; procurement and architecture decisions must decouple the two.

insights.claim: Scaling agentic AI differs from prior tech waves because anyone, not just software engineers, can build agents, and because services shift from deterministic with clear human-machine boundaries to a probabilistic pattern that defies traditional technology management.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: The democratization plus probabilistic combination is what breaks existing controls; the governance opportunity is structurally larger than for past IT waves.

insights.claim: The real governance problem with self-improving agents is drift management: agent behavior changes because context, accumulated memory, and occasional fine-tuning change, and that drift must be detected and controlled rather than celebrated as improvement.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Reframing self-improvement as drift points to a demand category for behavioral monitoring and version-controlled baselines, an emerging tooling market.
insights.quote: the governance problem is more accurately framed as drift management: Agents' behavior changes because context changes

insights.claim: A typical enterprise has 300 to 400 core processes and up to 1,300 subprocesses; if each subprocess is underpinned by five to seven transacting agents each consuming APIs, context stores, tools, and telemetry, the result is over 100,000 new technology assets.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: A two-orders-of-magnitude expansion in managed assets quantifies the addressable market for agent observability, registry, and governance tooling.
insights.quote: The result: over 100,000 new technology assets.

insights.claim: Even if only a third of processes are covered by agents (a typical midterm goal), the enterprise still faces thousands of agents spread across in-house, vendor, SaaS, and low-code platforms with logic that shifts as models and dependencies evolve.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Even conservative adoption produces unmanageable sprawl, so governance spend is not contingent on full agentic transformation.

insights.claim: Platform ecosystems like Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google Vertex already host agents in the hundreds of thousands, alongside a fast-growing tail of personal-use agents employees adopt outside any IT remit.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Shadow AI at the platform layer means enterprise guardrails must be embedded at the platform layer rather than imposed after the fact, favoring platform-native control vendors.

insights.claim: A fully agentic workflow can require around one million tokens per day, and in the 2028 scenario the cost base for 100,000 agents could shift materially overnight through a single vendor pricing decision or usage cap.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Concentrated token-pricing exposure recreates early-cloud cost unpredictability; FinOps for tokens and multi-provider hedging become material risk controls.
insights.quote: A fully agentic workflow can require around 10⁶ tokens per day.

themes.theme: How probabilistic systems break traditional controls

insights.claim: Agentic systems produce different outputs on identical inputs, both potentially correct, which breaks the deterministic foundation that makes regression testing, change management, and SLA contracts possible.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: SLAs must be reformulated as probabilistic guarantees (e.g. 95% of invocations within a tolerance band), with downstream implications for procurement, legal, and how businesses commit to outcomes.
insights.quote: Deterministic systems assume that identical inputs produce identical outputs

insights.claim: Agentic workflows tie business SLAs directly to technology SLAs, surfacing every underlying tech risk of an autonomously executing agent into the business and making Six Sigma-style resilience goals difficult when outputs vary on each run.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Operational risk migrates from IT into core business metrics, elevating agentic governance to a board-level rather than departmental concern.
insights.locator: The operational, compliance, and technical implications

insights.claim: Clean, accessible, well-governed data is a precondition for agentic success that most enterprises have not met, because agents reason over whatever data they can reach and produce confident outputs regardless of input quality.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Data platform maturity is the foundation the four pillars sit on, so data-quality and governance vendors are upstream beneficiaries of the agentic wave.
insights.quote: An enterprise with poor data architecture cannot succeed at agentic adoption no matter how mature its platform services or IT service management.

insights.claim: The proposed scalable AI backbone rests on four pillars: GenAI platform services, AI graduation pathways, continuous refactoring, and ITSM adapted for an Agent Development Life Cycle (ADLC), which treats the whole technology estate as a living product tracked through lineage, evaluation, and cost with guardrails-as-code.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: This is BCG's core prescriptive framework; it defines the categories of tooling and services enterprises will fund over the next 6-12 months.
insights.locator: ADLC treats the whole technology estate

insights.claim: The GenAI platform team must build a federated control fabric across walled-garden ecosystems so all agentic solutions operate within the same policy guardrails regardless of origin, with success measured by the proportion of solutions consuming platform services.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Interoperable, cross-ecosystem policy enforcement is the platform-services opportunity; single-ecosystem tools will be insufficient.
insights.locator: GenAI Platform Services: Building a Federated Control Fabric

insights.claim: Any agent that touches enterprise systems must be graduated into managed technology via a compact, machine-readable agent ID linking ownership, component inventories, data and model references, telemetry rollups, and a verified record of where it runs.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: The machine-readable agent identity is the linchpin artefact for traceability and incident response, a concrete standard vendors and internal teams will need to support.
insights.quote: Agentic solutions should not be treated exactly like technology assets nor like human users—they are a bit of both.

insights.claim: The agent ID lets the GenAI services platform team act as an immune system with funded authority to sweep the estate, onboard off-platform assets, and detect shadow IT, cost drift, and policy breaches, without becoming an adoption bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Success is measured by how fast an incident traces to the live agent instance that caused it, not by how many agent IDs are registered, a signal to watch when evaluating governance maturity.
insights.locator: The agent ID enables the GenAI

insights.claim: The most important graduation outcome is converting probabilistic agentic logic into deterministic code, alongside refining value proofs into enterprise-grade solutions or tightening agents within stronger guardrails.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Not everything should stay agentic; the ability to demote agents back to deterministic code is a maturity marker and a cost-control lever.
insights.quote: agentic logic is converted to deterministic code

insights.claim: A federated, business-unit-led operating model scales adoption faster than a central team, because making the enterprise depend on a stretched central GenAI team turns self-service into a shared-service bottleneck that breeds shadow IT.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Centralized AI governance can backfire; the operating-model choice materially affects adoption speed and control efficacy.

insights.claim: Companies must shift from SDLC, which governs discrete deterministic releases, to ADLC, which governs living adaptive systems that learn and change post-deployment, turning ITSM from a periodic control tower into a real-time safety system with guardrails-as-code and runtime enforcement.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Legacy ITSM vendors face a re-platforming pressure toward real-time, guardrails-as-code models; incumbents like ServiceNow must adapt or cede ground.

insights.claim: IT operations must move from a sample- and ticket-based model to continuous observability, with telemetry streaming in real time and mature AIOps handling first-line triage, because probabilistic systems behave differently on every run and waiting for a ticket is too late to catch drift.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: The shift is likened to a Formula 1 live-telemetry cockpit versus checking a car speedometer, pointing to demand for behavioral telemetry (prompts, model signatures, embeddings, action traces, cost tags, drift indicators).
insights.quote: The shift is less like checking the speedometer of a passenger car and more like the live telemetry cockpit running a Formula 1 race

insights.claim: ITSM should classify incidents by source (model, data, infrastructure, or the agent's own behavior) with predefined response steps and automatic containment mechanisms such as targeted kill switches, fallback routing to a safe-mode version, and circuit breakers.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: Source-classified incident response and automated containment define a concrete feature checklist for evaluating agentic ITSM tooling.

themes.theme: The closing window and C-suite mandate

insights.claim: CIOs have a six-to-twelve-month window to retrofit governance and architecture before agentic adoption outpaces their ability to manage it, after which today's investments harden into brittle legacy architecture.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: A defined near-term deadline creates urgency for backbone spend now; laggards face compounding remediation costs later.
insights.quote: We believe CIOs have a six- to twelve-month window before agentic complexity outpaces their ability to manage it.
insights.locator: The Window to Act Is Closing

insights.claim: Only around 15% of companies currently have this enterprise backbone in place, and the gap closes more slowly than agentic adoption grows.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: An 85% unmet-need figure sizes the runway for governance platforms and BCG-style advisory, while flagging a widening risk gap across the market.
insights.quote: only around 15% of companies have this enterprise backbone in place—and the gap closes more slowly than agentic adoption grows
insights.locator: To secure funding for this transition

insights.claim: To secure funding, CIOs should bring enterprise risk management and CFOs into the journey and reframe the ask around business risk mitigation and value delivery rather than warning about what might happen, which senior leaders find unpersuasive.
insights.speaker: Steven Kok
insights.so_what: The winning internal narrative is shared investment in enterprise resilience, not a technology request, a cue for how vendors should pitch buyers.
insights.locator: To secure funding for this transition
